key_insight: The paper shows that governing ODEs can be uniquely and stably recovered from solution trajectories only when they are sufficiently separated in the Hausdorff distance between solution sets.
engineering_relevance: This gives a theoretical foundation for data-driven system identification, helping engineers estimate how many trajectory observations are needed to reliably learn dynamics.
method: Define a Hausdorff distance on sets of ODE solution trajectories to compare equations via worst-case behavior over initial conditions.
Prove identifiability bounds for linear ODEs and nonlinear ODEs with Lipschitz/Hölder-continuous vector fields.
Derive metric entropy and sample-complexity bounds for recovering the governing equation from observed solutions.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical analysis only, with identifiability, metric entropy, and sample-complexity bounds but no numerical experiments.
watch_item: The guarantees depend on strong regularity and observability assumptions, so practical performance may be weaker for noisy, sparse, or partially observed data.
paper_title: Recovering Governing Equations from Solution Data: Identifiability Bounds for Linear and Nonlinear ODEs
